title: Two is Better Than One
summary: The 2x2 matrix is not exactly a public policy framework. But it is an incredibly useful tool for scenario-planning in any field or work, including public policy. These matrices are quite commonplace — you'll find them in many consultancy reports and impact assessments.
use_cases: When planning under uncertainty with multiple important and unknown variables
When you need a structured way to generate and compare future scenarios
When a single-variable forecast is inadequate for the decision at hand
When US-China decoupling could unfold in multiple ways that affect India's trade choices
When a long-term infrastructure investment must survive multiple regulatory environments
When coalition negotiations depend on two independent uncertainties like fiscal space and fragmentation

title: Four Components of an Economic Strategy
summary: The essential elements that make an economic strategy coherent rather than a list of wishes.
use_cases: When assembling an economic strategy that mixes rhetoric and concrete measures
When diagnosing whether a problem needs a policy change or just a new programme
When a PLI scheme is announced without accompanying trade and labour policy changes
When distinguishing Aatmanirbhar Bharat as slogan from its actual policy content
When a reform succeeds because it changed rules, not just because it launched a scheme
When explaining why manufacturing underperformance cannot be solved by programmes alone

title: Hyper Multi-Objective Optimisation
summary: Why pursuing many policy goals at once is the structural cause of policy failure.
use_cases: When a single policy instrument is being asked to serve too many masters
When deciding which objectives to drop or assign to separate agencies
When India's tax code is being loaded with yet another exemption for yet another constituency
When MGNREGS is expected to deliver safety net, rural assets, and climate resilience simultaneously
When traffic police are supposed to enforce rules and manage congestion at the same time
When a reform proposes augmenting agencies rather than pruning objectives

title: All Things Governments Do
summary: A working taxonomy of state functions, beyond the textbook list.
use_cases: When choosing which government instrument to apply to a given policy problem
When evaluating whether a lighter-touch tool could achieve the same goal
When a government defaults to a ban or direct production without considering nudges or taxes
When rhetoric (category 2) is being used as a substitute for regulation or enforcement
When comparing the LPG direct benefit transfer (category 5) against previous direct production
When explaining why bans on pornography or alcohol produce substitution rather than compliance

title: Anticipating Unintended Policy Consequences
summary: Distinguishing the policy outcomes that could have been foreseen from those that genuinely could not.
use_cases: When reviewing a policy proposal for foreseeable side effects before launch
When distinguishing between unavoidable surprises and design negligence after a failure
When a government defends a failed policy by calling its consequences 'unintended'
When basic economic reasoning would have predicted a consequence that planners missed
When assigning blame after a policy failure: did they know, or couldn't they have known?
When a price cap, odd-even scheme, or ban produces predictable workarounds

title: No More COP-outs
summary: . One of these eight steps involves coming up with evaluation criteria to compare and judge possible solutions.
use_cases: When comparing competing policy solutions and need evaluation criteria
When climate or environmental impact must be weighed alongside other policy objectives
When an infrastructure project is cheapest but has the highest carbon footprint
When agricultural subsidies raise yields but increase methane or fertiliser emissions
When equity for the poor and low-emission mobility pull an urban transport scheme in opposite directions
When adding emissions as a fifth evaluation criterion alongside effectiveness, efficiency, equity, and feasibility

title: Public Sector Reform
summary: Strategies for making large public organisations work better.
use_cases: When restructuring a public organisation that both sets rules and delivers services
When deciding what to privatise, regulate, or retain in-house
When Air India privatisation transfers rowing but the Civil Aviation Ministry retains steering
When an organisation like the CBFC edits content (rowing) but should only license certifiers (steering)
When a central bank simultaneously sets monetary policy and operates the payments system
When patronage from rowing functions explains bureaucratic resistance to privatisation

title: Eight Things to Unlearn Before Learning Public Policy
summary: Common misconceptions that have to be cleared before frameworks become useful.
use_cases: When entering a new policy domain and need to clear common mental models first
When training new analysts or students in realistic policy thinking
When attributing every policy failure to corruption and missing design or capacity problems
When a newsroom or classroom defaults to 'more rules needed' after any scandal
When a short-term scheme announcement is mistaken for a long-term structural strategy
When technical expertise is treated as sufficient for policy success without political economy

title: Prisoner's Dilemma in Policy Engineering
summary: Game theory as a working tool for designing institutions, not just describing them.
use_cases: When a powerful stakeholder is blocking reform and persuasion has failed
When designing implementation rules that align self-interest with collective goals
When incumbents in a regulated profession control entry and block liberalisation
When a reform needs the resistance to become the mechanism for its own passage
When a credible external threat can flip each party's dominant strategy toward cooperation
When the Medical Council blocks medical education expansion to protect existing doctors

title: India's Three Dimensions of Decentralisation
summary: Anatomising decentralisation in Indian governance along functional, fiscal, and political dimensions.
use_cases: When diagnosing why local governments fail to deliver despite constitutional mandates
When designing reforms to devolve power across levels of government
When Bengaluru floods despite the 74th Amendment guaranteeing urban local body powers
When a municipal corporation has administrative but not fiscal decentralisation
When State Finance Commissions are too weak to enforce meaningful revenue sharing
When distinguishing deconcentration, delegation, and devolution in governance reform

title: Errors of Omission and Commission
summary: The symmetric mistakes a policy can make: doing the wrong thing, and failing to do the right thing.
use_cases: When designing eligibility criteria for a subsidy or welfare scheme
When deciding which targeting error is more tolerable in a given context
When Aadhaar-linked DBT excludes genuine beneficiaries and you need to name the trade-off
When a politician defends inclusion errors by pointing to visible excluded victims
When comparing universal versus targeted approaches to social protection
When a new identification technology promises to shrink commission errors but risks omissions

title: Good Policy Problem Definition
summary: Criteria that separate a usable problem statement from a hidden solution.
use_cases: When reframing a solution-first proposal into an honest problem statement
When testing whether a stated problem admits multiple mutually exclusive solutions
When a ministry frames the problem as 'teachers need higher pay' rather than 'children cannot read'
When checking that a problem statement is outcome-oriented, specific, and measurable
When a policy brief's recommendations follow inevitably from a poorly framed question
When using ASER data to reframe education debate away from inputs toward learning outcomes

title: Sunset Clauses
summary: Sunset rules essentially require that programs and/or regulations need to be reauthorised periodically. The default is that the sun sets on them after a period of time; they die.
use_cases: When designing regulations that should not persist beyond their purpose
When building in accountability and periodic review for new programmes
When existing rules have outlived their rationale but are hard to repeal
When pandemic emergency powers risk becoming permanent through regulatory inertia
When a reform opponent can be brought on board by offering a built-in review clause
When frequent government changes make legal uncertainty worse than a scheduled expiry

title: What Makes a Policy Chance Stick?
summary: A section of Punjab and Western Uttar Pradesh agricultural land owners is threatening another protest. This group is a legal guarantee for Minimum Support Price (MSP), debt waivers, pensions for fa...
use_cases: When trying to understand why some policy reforms last and others are reversed
When designing reforms that need to survive political transitions
When a reform succeeded on paper but failed to change ground realities
When farm laws are repealed and leave the domain worse than before
When sequencing a reform to front-load lock-in and self-reinforcing support
When assessing whether a celebrated big-bang reform has generated any sticky conditions yet

title: Public Policy Solutionism
summary: A critique of the instinct to reach for clean solutions in domains where they do not exist.
use_cases: When a policy debate has stalled between cynicism and naive optimism
When building a reform narrative that acknowledges trade-offs without surrendering to them
When 'it's too complex' becomes an excuse for endless analysis with no recommendation
When a scheme is declared a success before outcomes are measured
When climate change discourse oscillates between apocalyptic fatalism and silver-bullet techno-optimism
When you need a mindset that starts with the problem, tests multiple interventions, and scales what works

title: Policies vs Programmes vs Practices
summary: Why the distinction between intent, scheme design, and on-ground implementation matters for analysis.
use_cases: When predicting whether a task is suitable for the state's current capacity
When deciding whether to script, automate, or professionalise a public service
When explaining why India runs elections and polio drives well but fails at primary healthcare
When digitisation converts a high-discretion practice into a scripted, scalable programme
When Aadhaar-linked welfare turned a discretionary local decision into an automated programme
When a well-designed policy collapses because it required practice-level judgment at massive scale

title: Wicked Problems
summary: Some policy problems resist clean definition and have no single right answer — only better and worse responses.
use_cases: When a policy problem keeps resisting every solution tried so far
When different stakeholders define the problem in incompatible ways
When you need to explain why a seemingly straightforward reform keeps failing
When designing an iterative, learning-based approach instead of a one-shot solution
When deciding whether to decompose a big problem or tackle it holistically
When a policy proposal smuggles in a single solution as its problem statement

title: A COVID-19 Vaccine Deployment Strategy for India
summary: What should India's approach be to deploying a COVID-19 vaccine? Once a vaccine candidate passes all clinical trial stages, the sequencing problem is non-trivial for a country of India's size and income levels.
use_cases: When designing rollout plans for large-scale public health interventions
When deciding how to sequence a scarce resource across population groups
When state delivery capacity is the binding constraint, not supply
When randomisation is faster and fairer than fine-grained demographic sequencing at scale
When repurposing Election Commission booth infrastructure for mission-mode health delivery
When a supply-constrained rollout requires diversified sourcing and transparent procurement

title: One Instrument, One Target
summary: Each policy goal needs its own dedicated tool. Stacking many goals onto one instrument is how policies fail at all of them.
use_cases: When a single agency or law is being loaded with multiple conflicting goals
When redesigning accountability so that success can be clearly measured
When diagnosing why an income tax, police force, or welfare scheme fails at everything
When deciding whether to subtract objectives from an overburdened institution or create new ones
When a reform produces litigation and evasion because too many objectives distort the incentive
When comparing two policy options and checking whether each has a dedicated instrument

title: Understanding Corruption
summary: A structured way to examine corruption beyond the moralising frame.
use_cases: When designing anti-corruption strategy tailored to the dominant local form
When explaining why corruption persists despite moral condemnation
When speed money for driving licences and building permits is the dominant Indian form
When comparing India's petty-bribe culture with China's access-money infrastructure model
When a growing economy transitions from speed money to access money and the stakes change
When anti-corruption campaigns focus on morality without changing the underlying incentive structure

title: When Conditions Become Policy Problems
summary: The process by which a fact about the world acquires the status of a policy problem requiring action.
use_cases: When trying to move a chronic issue onto the political agenda
When analysing how a problem definition was manufactured or contested
When Delhi's air quality became a judicially enforced problem after WHO ranked it worst globally
When ease-of-doing-business indices converted a structural condition into a political pressure
When the category a problem is placed in determines which ministry and tools respond
When a condition is reframed from a 'social issue' to a 'macroeconomic constraint' to unlock resources

title: Seven Stages of the Policy Pipeline
summary: From a fact about the world to a working law on the ground, every policy passes through seven distinct stages — each requiring its own kind of state capacity.
use_cases: When diagnosing why a well-intentioned reform collapsed after announcement
When planning long-term investments in statistics, research, and legal capacity
When a government leaps to legislation without upstream evidence or alternatives
When a state's court challenges reveal thin legal drafting capacity at stage 6
When deciding where in the pipeline to intervene as a think tank or researcher
When justifying why research, statistics bureaus, and public debate are not luxuries

title: Complexity and Public Policy
summary: How to think about policy in systems where outcomes do not follow linearly from inputs.
use_cases: When adapting a 'best practice' from another country to local institutional conditions
When designing policies for domains with strong feedback loops and emergent behaviour
When someone argues India should copy Seoul's chaebols or Amsterdam's bicycle lanes
When a policy produced unexpected systemic effects that no blueprint could have predicted
When justifying why decentralised experimentation beats centrally designed blueprints
When initial conditions — not recipes — are the lever worth pulling

title: Opportunity Cost Neglect
summary: Why policy debates systematically forget what could have been done with the same resources.
use_cases: When comparing a proposed new scheme against alternative uses of the same funds
When explaining why subsidy debates ignore the cost to excluded groups
When a budget adds a new programme without asking what existing programme it replaces
When middle-class LPG or railway subsidies persist despite regressive incidence
When a Finance Commission allocation is the only moment opportunity costs become explicit
When a public health programme is evaluated in isolation, not against alternative interventions

title: Taxonomy of Policy Failures and Successes
summary: A typology that separates kinds of policy outcomes — design failures, implementation failures, and so on.
use_cases: When a policy has failed and you need a structured way to diagnose why
When separating political success from programmatic success in a review
When rent control is electorally popular but demonstrably destroys housing supply
When a technically sound pension reform is reversed because of coalition politics
When distinguishing a theory-of-change failure from an implementation failure
When a policy followed the right process but produced wrong incentives on the ground

title: Outlays, Outputs, Outcomes
summary: Money spent is not the same as work done, and work done is not the same as lives changed. Track all three.
use_cases: When building a monitoring framework for a government scheme
When diagnosing whether a scheme failed on implementation or on theory of change
When a budget debate conflates spending levels with actual improvement in lives
When a minister claims success by citing disbursements but not outcomes
When evaluating whether high school-enrolment numbers mean children are learning
When constructing a results framework for a new programme from scratch

title: Nine Competing Visions of Equality
summary: Distinct, often incompatible, conceptions of what equality means in policy debate.
use_cases: When designing a distributive scheme and need to justify who gets what
When stakeholders disagree on what 'fair' means in a resource allocation
When caste-based reservation is challenged as 'unequal treatment of equals'
When a government distributes ₹50,000 crore and must choose among nine defensible methods
When a policy debate on equity conflates group-based distribution with merit-based allocation
When you need to show that every distribution is unequal on some dimension

title: Types of Causal Narratives
summary: The story-shapes through which policy problems are explained — and which shape wins shapes the response.
use_cases: When crafting or critiquing the public story around a policy proposal
When diagnosing which causal story a government is deploying to deflect blame
When stubble burning is framed as a weather phenomenon rather than a policy failure
When demonetisation's failures are explained as unguided forces rather than foreseeable negligence
When competing causal stories about Delhi's air pollution predict different policy responses
When assigning responsibility for a policy outcome requires choosing a causal story type

title: What Makes a Good Narrative?
summary: Characteristics of policy stories that travel: simplicity, emotional pull, and a credible villain.
use_cases: When crafting a policy narrative that needs to travel beyond expert circles
When critiquing a well-packaged but harmful policy that has a compelling story
When Aatmanirbhar Bharat is the narrative and you need to understand why it works regardless of economics
When evidence-based arguments are losing to a story that satisfies all five elements
When building a counter-narrative against import protectionism or climate fatalism
When a slow-burn structural issue like pension reform resists compelling storytelling

title: The Purva Paksha Debate
summary: roughly uses this style—Kautilya first lists the best form of opposing arguments before offering his own. I find that Ambedkar’s writing also often adopts this style.
use_cases: When preparing to argue a contested policy position and need to steelman the opposition first
When a policy debate is generating more heat than light
When reviewing a position paper — strengthen the opposing view before dismantling it
When building institutional cultures that reward intellectual honesty over rhetorical dominance
When training analysts to interrogate their own assumptions before critiquing a policy
When structuring red-teaming or peer review in a policy organisation

title: Stakeholder Management in Public Policy
summary: The practical work of building support for a reform, one stakeholder at a time.
use_cases: When a reform has sound economics but no strategy for moving key opponents before the bill drops
When building a stakeholder map to decide who needs to be managed closely vs kept satisfied
When GST-style reform requires moving 29 state governments from opponent to supporter
When farm law failure shows the cost of underestimating a high-power, high-interest stakeholder
When a low-power stakeholder acquires high power overnight through viral mobilisation
When defining what you need from each stakeholder: consent, silence, resources, or active advocacy

title: Political Policymaking
summary: How political incentives shape what policy can be made, beyond the technocratic frame.
use_cases: When a reform document must explicitly separate which measures address immediate flows vs structural stocks
When PLI schemes are announced and the question is whether the structural reforms will follow
When a crisis creates political space for stock reform that normal times cannot
When a government only delivers flow wins and leaves structural problems untouched
When an analyst dismisses a flow measure without recognising its political function
When sequencing reforms so flow wins generate the mandate for harder stock changes

title: The Overton Window
summary: On any issue, only a narrow band of positions is considered politically acceptable. The band moves.
use_cases: When assessing whether a proposed policy is sayable in the current climate or will be dismissed as fringe
When designing long-term advocacy that aims to shift acceptable opinion before seeking adoption
When explaining why Air India privatisation was untenable in 2000 but passed easily in 2021
When choosing whether to target radical positions to drag the centre toward your preferred outcome
When a policy is correct but outside the window — and you need to plan the acceptability work first
When different language communities have different windows on the same policy question

title: Why Weak Dictators Get Softer Loans
summary: The political economy of international lending to authoritarian regimes.
use_cases: When a domestically fragile leader extracts better international terms by citing ratification constraints
When explaining why Bhutto got generous Shimla terms despite Pakistan losing the war
When India used IMF conditions at Level 1 to build domestic support for Level 2 liberalisation
When a trade negotiation breaks down because the domestic win-set shifted during talks
When a social media Level 3 constrains what governments can agree at the diplomatic table
When predicting whether an adversary's domestic fragility is a real constraint or a negotiating tactic

title: Rules For Political Survival
summary: : Keep your winning coalition as small as possible. A small coalition allows a leader to rely on very few people to stay in power. Fewer essentials equals more control and contributes to more discretion over expenditures.
use_cases: When analysing why a leader resists reforms that would benefit the population broadly
When trying to predict how an authoritarian or democratic government will respond to pressure
When patronage transfers persist even when public goods would deliver higher welfare
When disaster relief is captured by political elites rather than delivered to affected citizens
When reading coalition politics in Indian states where patronage networks determine outcomes
When designing institutional reforms that expand the winning coalition's incentives toward public goods

title: Confronting Trade-offs
summary: Methods for reasoning when no policy choice can satisfy everyone.
use_cases: When forced to choose between competing policy objectives with no clean win-win
When making or justifying a policy choice under uncertainty or competing pressures
When a USB-C charger mandate claims environmental benefits but forecloses innovation
When import tariffs justified by self-reliance must be tested against consumer and efficiency costs
When a loan waiver or farm subsidy needs its interests alignment and capacity steps checked
When a proposed ban ignores whether those bearing costs have incentives to evade it

title: Cognitive Maps
summary: Why two informed people can reach opposing conclusions from the same facts — and what to do about it.
use_cases: When two equally informed experts reach opposite conclusions from the same evidence
When a reform fails because it changed rules without changing how opponents understood the problem
When the NPS reform needed to exclude existing employees to align their map with the change
When states resisted GST until a five-year revenue guarantee shifted their map of the transition
When negotiation fails because one side misreads what the other side is actually optimising for
When concessions need to be real, not cosmetic, to avoid the trust collapse of fake alignment

title: Kingdon's Three Streams
summary: Reform happens when a problem, a solution, and a political moment line up at the same time. Until they do, no amount of effort is enough.
use_cases: When a long-recognised problem suddenly becomes actionable after years of neglect
When planning reform advocacy by identifying whether all three streams are ripe for coupling
When explaining why crisis moments produce policy changes that decades of deliberation could not
When a think tank must have solutions ready before the political window opens
When a new government's first year is the widest window and reformers must be prepared
When a policy stalls because a mature problem lacks a developed and available solution

title: Wilson's Interest Group Matrix
summary: Whether a policy gets passed depends less on its merits than on how its costs and benefits are distributed.
use_cases: When predicting whether a reform will face organised opposition or diffuse indifference
When designing sequencing or compensation to shift a proposal from a hostile quadrant to a passable one
When diagnosing why economically sensible reforms stall while inefficient subsidies persist
When fertiliser subsidies survive every government because concentrated farmers beat dispersed taxpayers
When privatisation requires a political entrepreneur willing to spend capital against organised unions
When GST took a decade because gains were too dispersed to mobilise champions early

title: Marginal Cost of Public Finance
summary: Every additional rupee government raises has an economic cost beyond the rupee itself — and that cost matters.
use_cases: When evaluating whether keeping Air India, BSNL, or a loss-making PSU under state control passes the social cost test
When a ₹1 lakh crore fertiliser subsidy must be tested against the ₹3 lakh crore cost society actually bears
When comparing cash transfers to in-kind subsidies and need to show the latter imposes higher distortion
When arguing for GST broadening or rate rationalisation as a way to lower the MCPF across all spending
When a new government programme promises social returns and you need a threshold to test whether they clear the bar
When a Pigouvian carbon tax is proposed and you need to explain why its MCPF can fall below 1

title: A useful thumb rule:
summary: the cost to society for every rupee of public spending in India is around ₹3. Therefore, the government should only spend an additional one rupee when the gains to society from that action exceed ₹3.
use_cases: When evaluating whether a proposed public expenditure is worth its cost to society
When comparing government provision with private or market alternatives
When making the case against a subsidy or public programme that fails a cost-benefit test
When deciding whether to continue running a loss-making PSU or exit
When a ₹1 lakh crore fertiliser subsidy must be tested against a ₹3 lakh crore social cost
When arguing for tax simplification as a way to lower the MCPF and expand fiscal space

title: A Taxing Month Ahead
summary: What’s a budget without new taxes, fees, cesses, or surcharges? As the budget date nears, let’s understand the categorical differences between these four seemingly interchangeable terms.
use_cases: When analysing the fiscal implications of a new cess or surcharge
When a Union budget expands cesses to grow revenue outside the divisible pool
When explaining the difference between a tax, fee, cess, and surcharge
When advising state governments on raising user-charge revenue without central encroachment
When diagnosing why states' share of actual devolution is lower than the Finance Commission formula implies
When a new education cess reveals the state has failed to use existing taxes for their stated purpose

title: Algorithm for Fiscal Federalism
summary: A systematic approach to splitting revenue and responsibility between centre and states.
use_cases: When states' share in spending has grown but revenue share has not kept pace
When Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, and Telangana protests about budget allocation are symptoms of vertical imbalance
When cesses and surcharges are expanding outside the divisible pool without states noticing
When a Finance Commission reforms horizontal devolution but leaves vertical devolution unchanged
When Centrally Sponsored Schemes distort state priorities by requiring unaffordable matching funds
When sequencing fiscal federalism reforms from most to least beneficial for states

title: Wicksellian Disconnection
summary: The mismatch between who pays for a service and who consumes it, and the political pathologies it creates.
use_cases: When municipal roads remain broken despite rising local tax burdens — showing payer, decider, and beneficiary are three different circles
When state governments offer free electricity or water waivers without bearing the fiscal cost themselves
When comparing Surat's functional municipal finance against a city where state transfers replace local revenue
When arguing for value-based property taxes or user charges as a way to restore local fiscal accountability
When a 74th Amendment reform fails because local bodies have devolved responsibilities but no devolved revenue
When evaluating whether a Central Sponsored Scheme weakens local accountability by bypassing the Wicksellian link

title: The Domar Rule for Public Debt Sustainability
summary: Public debt is sustainable as long as the economy grows faster than the rate of interest on the debt.
use_cases: When assessing whether a country's public debt trajectory is sustainable over the long run
When austerity is proposed and you need to test whether it worsens the debt by crushing growth
When post-pandemic bond yields approach nominal GDP growth and fiscal space tightens
When evaluating whether borrowing for climate infrastructure or human capital can pay for itself
When state government debt looks alarming in absolute terms but stable when r < g holds
When distinguishing borrowing for productive investment from borrowing for subsidies that don't lift growth

title: Three Functions of the State
summary: Every government does three economic jobs at once — provide what markets won't, redistribute, and stabilise the macroeconomy. Confusion between them is a leading cause of bad policy.
use_cases: When fuel subsidies distort fuel prices and you need to show they are simultaneously failing on allocation, distribution, and stabilisation
When LPG direct benefit transfer is proposed as replacing a price subsidy — explaining why DBT separates the three functions cleanly
When a policy debate conflates 'government should provide this' (allocation) with 'government should subsidise the poor' (distribution)
When MGNREGS is justified as a stabiliser but evaluated only on distributive outcomes
When a finance commission must decide which level of government should bear which function
When diagnosing why a single instrument — a subsidy, a tax, a scheme — is colliding across multiple functions

title: What Global Order Are We In?
summary: A framework for naming the current international system — unipolar, multipolar, or something messier.
use_cases: When placing the current world in the 'Great Walls' scenario — stagnated global economy plus intensifying US-China conflict — and drawing out its implications
When the Russia-Ukraine war sharpens bifurcation between the West and the Russia-China axis and India is caught between military dependence and strategic convergence
When India is pushed to 'strategic autonomy by compulsion' in a Great Walls world and needs to identify small-bet investments that hedge across scenarios
When diagnosing whether the world is unipolar, multipolar, or fragmented along geoeconomic lines using the two-axis scenario matrix
When global bodies lose relevance and state-centric institutions become more important — understanding this as a feature of the Great Walls scenario
When advising which international institutions India should invest in for influence given the shift from multilateralism to state-centric power blocs

title: Three Schools of Thought on India–US Relations
summary: The competing perspectives Indian strategists hold on the bilateral relationship.
use_cases: When mapping whether an Indian foreign policy commentator belongs to the retaliatory, bandwagoning, or swing-power school — and what each implies
When the retaliatory school's 'why support the US when they back Pakistan' argument needs to be tested against opportunity cost
When the swing-power strategy is constrained by insufficient Indian power and the question is how much power growth changes the calculation
When intensifying US-China rivalry is closing the swing option and India must decide whether to move closer to a bandwagoning posture
When the highest common factor across all three schools — rapid Indian power growth — is used to find political common ground in the debate
When advising on whether India's current policy (closer to swing) is the optimal choice given the current power differential with both superpowers

title: Describing State Policy on Geopolitical Issues
summary: A common vocabulary for analysing what a state's position on any given foreign-policy issue actually is.
use_cases: When analysing India's Israel-Palestine position — mapping interests (Gulf energy, Israeli tech), actions (UN votes, bilateral visits), and effects (preserved ties, no mediating role)
When China's Afghanistan policy needs analysis that avoids both demonisation and apologia by applying the three-step protocol
When cutting through diplomatic rhetoric to identify what a state has actually done versus what it claims its position is
When India's abstentions at the UN Security Council need to be evaluated as deliberate calibrations, not mere fence-sitting
When assessing whether a state's actions have exposed its own weaknesses even while opening new opportunities
When an op-ed attributes a state's foreign policy to ideology or hypocrisy without running the interests-actions-effects protocol

title: Responding to LAC Standoff in Ladakh
summary: A working framework for thinking through India's options on a live border crisis.
use_cases: When China has seized territory at the LAC and India's task is harder than China's because it seeks to restore status quo ante, not just hold
When evaluating the 'replicate and negotiate' option — seizing a comparable position elsewhere to create bargaining leverage
When assessing whether diplomatic de-escalation is viable if China has no incentive to give up gains without a corresponding concession
When deciding whether to continue the standoff (holding pattern, no reversal), escalate, or negotiate in a localised military crisis
When the tactical question is whether India has locations along the LAC where it can seize and hold ground with manageable risk
When accompanying escalation with clear diplomatic messaging about the path back to mutual de-escalation — avoiding runaway escalation

title: Decoupling Dynamics
summary: The mechanics of economic-political separation between major economies.
use_cases: When mapping how US-China separation is proceeding differently across critical inputs (semiconductors), data governance, and financial flows
When India is deciding where to focus its China-decoupling opportunity — critical inputs, digital governance, or standards-setting
When a supply chain is claimed to be 'decoupling' and you need to check whether it is the financial, trade, innovation, or digital layer that is actually moving
When warning against the assumption that supply chains will automatically relocate to India without India actively competing for them
When China restricts rare earth exports and a Quad Critical Minerals Initiative is proposed — placing both moves in the nine-layer framework
When financial decoupling is overstated because China still depends on the US dollar for Belt and Road project financing

title: Human Capital Investment Model for National Security
summary: Defence capability is built from people, not platforms; the framework forces that fact into view.
use_cases: When defence pensions (25 paise per rupee) exceed weapons procurement (22 paise) and the reform must avoid France's 2020 sequencing mistakes
When OROP has doubled India's defence pension bill to ₹1.12 lakh crore and the NPS-for-new-recruits route faces political resistance
When proposing lateral movement of military personnel into paramilitary, police, and intelligence agencies to extend service years outside the pension system
When designing a transition that protects existing retirees while changing the structure for new entrants — replicating the 2004 civilian NPS masterstroke
When the net present value of defence pension liabilities is estimated at 50 per cent of GDP and weapons procurement is being crowded out
When assessing whether common training standards and transferable service records can enable cross-organisational mobility at scale

title: What Makes an Asset Strategic?
summary: The criteria that separate genuinely strategic assets from things merely labelled strategic.
use_cases: When semiconductors need to be justified as strategic: high importance, high externality, high nationalisation — strong on all three logics
When rare earths are labelled strategic but processing is the bottleneck, not mining — shifting where India's investment should target
When AI research is proposed as a national security priority and the question is whether open publication undermines the nationalisation criterion
When separating sectors that deserve strategic investment from those labelled strategic purely for protectionist purposes
When designing a Critical Minerals Initiative and needing to rank the dependency-strategic, infrastructure-strategic, and cumulative-strategic logics for each mineral
When a policy memo labels an industry as 'strategic' without specifying whether the externality accrues to India or diffuses to rivals

title: Guns and Butter
summary: The classic trade-off between defence and civilian spending, applied to Indian budgetary politics.
use_cases: When the defence-vs-health debate frames the budget as zero-sum and needs reframing around the 18 per cent of spending that goes to neither
When India's defence pension bill (25 paise per rupee) exceeds weapons procurement (22 paise) and structural reform is overdue
When the railways' two-thirds-of-budget pay-and-pension drain is cited as the real crowd-out, not guns vs butter
When COVID-19 framing pits public health against defence spending and you need the Kennedy-ian counter-argument
When identifying which expenditures crowd out both security and civilian investment — irrational overheads, not the other's budget
When assessing whether India is at the frontier where guns and butter trade off, or still below it where both need more investment

title: How Democratic States Perceive Each Other
summary: The structured way democracies read other democracies — and where the reading breaks down.
use_cases: When India-US state-to-state ties strengthen but Indian public opinion on the US stays sceptical — diagnosing the weak state-to-people pillar
When high-level diplomacy and diaspora warmth do not automatically translate into public trust between two democracies
When India Twitter's anti-American sentiment drowns out the 64 per cent of non-expert respondents who rate India-US as the most important bilateral
When designing pre-emptive briefing and messaging strategies that address the state-to-people axis before negative coverage sets the narrative
When explaining why authoritarian states have an asymmetric advantage: what their citizens think of foreign countries cannot constrain their foreign policy
When a bilateral relationship needs the three-pillar diagnostic to identify which combination of state-to-state, state-to-people, and people-to-people investment is most underfunded

title: India and the Post-COVID-19 World Order
summary: How the pandemic accelerated geopolitical realignments India has to navigate.
use_cases: When placing India in the 'Race to the Bottom' scenario and identifying which domestic reforms must accompany the external realignment
When identifying dominant strategy moves — domestic manufacturing, middle-power ties, resilient supply chains — that pay off under multiple geopolitical futures
When the Russia-Ukraine war pushes India toward strategic autonomy by compulsion and the framework helps distinguish small bets from all-in alignment
When advising India to invest in relationships with Vietnam, Indonesia, Australia, and UAE as scenario-robust moves
When a weakened US and a rising China both court middle powers — mapping India's swing-power and closer-alignment options
When the liberal economic order is under threat and India's growth engine needs a manufacturing anchor to compensate for thinning capital flows

title: Dictatorship and Democracy in Israel and Pakistan
summary: A comparative reading of two states often analogised — and what the analogy hides.
use_cases: When explaining why Israel sustained democracy and Pakistan did not, despite both being religious minority states with strong security establishments
When assessing how Ben-Gurion's coup-proofing measures and the Muslim League's weak institutionalisation produced opposite civil-military outcomes
When designing institutional safeguards in a newly formed state during the formative decade when civil-military paths are set
When explaining why Bangladesh managed a more durable democratic practice than Pakistan despite inheriting the same military DNA
When a fragile democracy faces a strong military and the question is whether the formative-year institutional choices are still reversible
When warning that Pakistan's democratic struggles are not merely about present-day leaders but partly determined by 1947-57 decisions

title: Aap Hamaare Hain Kaun, Russia?
summary: An honest framework for India's actual strategic relationship with Russia.
use_cases: When evaluating whether India's $10 billion annual defence trade concentration with Russia still serves national interest
When the 'strategic autonomy' argument is invoked to resist closer US alignment — testing it against the reliability-perception matrix
When assessing whether the 1971 Soviet deployment meets the strictest test of reliability: self-harming action in India's interest
When designing a rebalancing strategy that diversifies defence procurement away from a single-track partner
When Russia-Ukraine tensions force India to confront what the partnership actually delivers beyond the defence trade lane
When partners with whom India has broad trade relations (US, France, Israel) are compared to Russia as sources of defence equipment

title: Ingredients of a New World Order
summary: The components — power, ideology, institutions — that define an international system.
use_cases: When explaining why US withdrawal from TPP and the Iran deal eroded its legitimacy faster than any loss of material power
When assessing why China's power rivals the US but its hierarchical worldview — 'civilisation vs non-civilisation' — makes Pax Sinica repulsive to most states
When India's cosmopolitan culture and pluralism are identified as legitimacy assets that can compensate for its power deficit in international institution-building
When diagnosing an existing international order's weakness by separating the decline of accepted rules from the decline of the underlying power balance
When a rising power claims global leadership and the question is whether it has the legitimacy, not just the material capacity, to hold it
When Kissinger's balance between power and legitimacy is needed to judge whether a state's conduct is heading toward crusade or impotent policy

title: What Made the US Enable China's Rise
summary: A historical reading of strategic miscalculation by an open economy facing a closed rival.
use_cases: When explaining why US support for China's WTO entry was not a conspiracy or blunder but a structurally predictable short-term-horizon choice
When China's 'hide and bide' long-term focus met the US's short-term focus on cheap manufacturing — cooperation was the equilibrium
When China's aggression toward neighbours after 2010 signalled a shift to short-term horizons and US preventive confrontation became the equilibrium
When calibrating India's own China policy by asking whether China is on a short-term or long-term horizon in a specific domain
When warning that a return to long-term Chinese focus — 'biding its time' — could shift the equilibrium back toward cooperation regardless of stated US policy
When diagnosing incumbent-great-power miscalculation by identifying the time-horizon mismatch that made co-operation rational in the moment

title: A Taxonomy of Defence Innovation
summary: On November 30th, the Defence Ministry approved IAF's capital acquisition proposal for 97 Tejas Mk1A aircraft. This move signals a major shift — India's armed forces have accepted the Tejas platform as a replacement for their inventory of old and outdated, mostly Russian, aircraft.
use_cases: When diagnosing why India's Tejas programme advanced slowly despite a clear need — identifying which of seven factors was binding
When comparing India's incremental catch-up regime against China's rapidly-catching-up regime across input, organisational, and catalytic factors
When evaluating a defence indigenisation policy and identifying whether the constraint is budget, technology transfer, organisational, or institutional
When the IAF accepts Tejas Mk1A and the question is whether it signals a systemic shift or a one-off procurement decision
When a new DPP or defence innovation fund is announced and needs to be mapped to the taxonomy before its likely impact can be assessed
When advising on whether private-sector and startup-driven defence innovation can substitute for state-led organisational reform

title: Instruments of Technology Geopolitics
summary: This shift explains why a DeepSeek came about after OpenAI blocked access to Chinese users and the US government blocked Chinese users from accessing advanced chips.
use_cases: When US chip export controls accelerate China's domestic DeepSeek development — diagnosing the substitution effect of technology denial
When China restricts rare earth magnets and the Quad responds with a Critical Minerals Initiative — placing both moves in the instruments framework
When deciding whether India's semiconductor strategy should prioritise export controls, investment screening, or standards-setting as its primary tool
When assessing whether a technology export control will constrain a rival or merely accelerate their development of alternatives
When reading the US-China tech war as a dynamic game where each denial move produces a substitution response, not a permanent freeze
When mapping which instruments — from data localisation to procurement preferences — are available to a middle power like India in technology statecraft

title: India's Approach Towards Chinese Firms
summary: A framework for balancing technology, security, and economic openness in dealing with Chinese companies.
use_cases: When the Huawei and ZTE 5G ban needs to be justified on strategic criticality and information-space grounds, not blanket nationalism
When solar panel imports from China require a decision on whether low-criticality and low-Indian-capacity justifies a tariff or local-content rule
When machine tool imports face a 'strategic' label and the framework forces an explicit debate on importance, externality, and nationalisation
When Chinese overcapacity threatens to decimate Indian firms in a sector and the Indian capacity question must be answered using export performance, not domestic market share
When deciding whether to welcome, regulate, or ban Chinese investment based on the 2x2 of strategic criticality and existing Indian capacity
When a blanket ban or blanket openness is proposed and the framework insists on sector-by-sector calibration across investments, talent, and imports

title: Paradoxes of India's Westernophobia
summary: Contradictions in how India relates to Western powers — economically, strategically, and rhetorically.
use_cases: When confronting the Vishwaguru-Victim paradox: India cannot simultaneously claim geopolitical indispensability and argue it is too weak to side with the West
When testing whether India Twitter's Westernophobia represents broader Indian opinion — showing the 64 per cent who rate India-US as the top bilateral
When the 'India-Russia partnership weans Russia from China' argument needs to be deflated using GDP-per-capita and trade-structure data
When Russia-Ukraine tensions force India's foreign policy establishment to confront outdated lessons drawn from 1971 and Afghanistan
When emotional or historical barriers are preventing closer US alignment and the three paradoxes need to be surfaced and named
When the US can reduce India's Westernophobia by backing UNSC inclusion, reducing technology transfer barriers, and deepening the technology alliance

title: Flakiness in US Pakistan Policy
summary: A pattern-level analysis of the inconsistencies in American policy towards Pakistan.
use_cases: When predicting that a US training programme suspension will eventually reverse because the military-jihadi complex will manufacture a new crisis of relevance
When India lobbies for a permanent US crackdown on Pakistan and needs to understand why structural incentives make that bet unreliable
When mapping the five-stage cycle: crisis → US funding → Pakistan leverage → US discovery → sanctions → next crisis
When designing India's independent countermeasures that are not hostage to the US-Pakistan reset cycle
When explaining why US-Pakistan bonhomie survives repeated betrayals — the structural demand for Pakistan's geostrategic position persists
When assessing whether India-US cooperation should be conditioned on US behaviour toward Pakistan or designed to be independent of it

title: National Security Preparedness and COVID-19 Planning
summary: Why pandemics belong in national security thinking, not just public health.
use_cases: When COVID-19 lockdown management and the LAC standoff simultaneously compete for police capacity — identifying policing as the critical bottleneck
When a single development (pandemic) generates multiple cascading risks across health, internal security, and border management simultaneously
When distinguishing preparedness actions (taken in advance to reduce likelihood) from countermeasures (taken after a risk materialises) in a contingency plan
When proposing to surge policing capacity through Central Armed Police Forces, private security offloading, or retired officers during a crisis
When climate disasters, cyber-attacks, or economic shocks generate cross-silo cascading risks that departmental plans have not anticipated
When designing national security preparedness plans that are generic enough to handle unexpected developments, not only pre-specified scenarios

title: Hypocrisy in International Relations
summary: A typology of inconsistent state behaviour — and why some hypocrisies are stable and others are not.
use_cases: When India is accused of hypocrisy on non-alignment, climate, or democracy — explaining the charge sticks because of weakness, not unique inconsistency
When the US attacked Iraq under a democracy pretext and the framework explains why the hypocrisy was unlabelled at the time
When deciding whether to go on the defensive about a hypocrisy accusation or to invest in power so the charge becomes unenforceable
When explaining why high-power states can universalise their own moral claims while low-power states cannot manage the same gap between rhetoric and action
When Nordic countries' high moral standing despite low power needs to be explained as an exception to the realist account
When a domestic public demands foreign-policy consistency that is strategically costly — using the framework to explain why the best strategy is artful inconsistency

title: China's Predicament
summary: The structural strategic challenges China faces — and what they imply for India.
use_cases: When explaining why Chinese aggression in the Himalayas and South China Sea is structurally driven, not merely diplomatic bad faith
When designing India's strategy against an adversary that is simultaneously powerful enough to absorb pain and dependent enough to be vulnerable in supply chains
When China's semiconductor import dependence creates leverage that India and Quad partners can exploit in critical mineral and maritime domains
When forecasting that Chinese external aggression will increase rather than decrease as its domestic economic performance weakens
When placing China's current behaviour in its 2x2 of power and world-dependence — the unprecedented high-power, high-dependence quadrant
When assessing whether better diplomacy alone can change Chinese behaviour or whether structural drivers must be addressed

title: Why We Do Stupid Things in Groups
summary: A reading of collective irrationality that takes the social mechanics seriously.
use_cases: When Kumbh pilgrims gather during a pandemic and you need to explain the behaviour as group-level selection, not individual irrationality
When mob violence damages the rioters' own neighbourhoods and the explanation requires going beyond individual rational choice
When religious congregations hold collective prayer despite infection risk — understanding it as evolved group-binding behaviour
When predicting that group solidarity will override individual self-interest in a collective decision-making context
When designing public health messaging during a crisis that must account for the evolved pull of group-binding norms over individual risk calculus
When assessing whether a harmful collective behaviour can be redirected or must be displaced by a competing group-binding ritual

title: We Hate Cognitive Dissonance
summary: (based on gut feelings). It's an uncomfortable thought, hang on.
use_cases: When a well-evidenced farm law or economic reform is rejected by those whose intuitions precede their reasoning
When designing public health messaging that must work with the messenger and framing, not just the facts
When explaining why political opponents are not missing information — they are filtering it through different intuitions
When a policy argument is logically sound but audiences still reject it because intuition trumps reasoning
When auditing your own policy position to separate genuine reasoning from post-hoc justification
When persuading a coalition partner whose group norms have already predetermined their stance

title: The State and the Society
summary: A framework for the relationship between the two — neither extension nor opposite.
use_cases: When Church Street Bengaluru's citizen-managed upgrade degrades after BBMP takes over — diagnosing society substitution vs co-production
When an NGO runs a public school and the state stops hiring teachers because 'it's covered'
When assessing whether a civil society intervention is building state capacity or enabling permanent abdication
When applying Ostrom's eight design principles to diagnose why a lake, forest, or neighbourhood commons management collapsed
When designing TenderSURE-style urban infrastructure to ensure the template trains state engineers rather than bypasses them
When Swachh Bharat campaign energy fails to translate into sustained sanitation because state maintenance capacity was never built

title: The Basis of Morality
summary: The frameworks within which moral claims become arguable, rather than just felt.
use_cases: When farm law debates talk past each other because one side experiences them through Care and the other through Liberty and Sanctity
When crafting persuasive communication across political divides by mapping which of six moral foundations each audience weighs most
When citizenship, personal law, or reservation debates feel irresolvable despite both sides citing facts — the conflict is in foundations, not evidence
When designing messaging for a policy that must appeal to both liberal (Care/Fairness) and conservative (Authority/Sanctity) stakeholders
When a red-teaming exercise needs to represent an opposing position as a genuine moral position, not mere ignorance
When explaining why bombarding an audience with data fails when their intuition has already determined the conclusion

title: Three Truths of Ideology
summary: The principles that animate how ideologies actually function — beyond their stated beliefs.
use_cases: When an opposition coalition defines itself only as 'not the current government' and you need to diagnose its missing sacred core
When an ideological movement's claims expand from temples to textbooks to trade and internal contradictions begin to appear
When 'anti-national' labels, expanded ideological shadows, and sanctified symbols all appear — mapping each to its truth
When Reagan-Thatcher conservatism fractures under the weight of culture wars, deregulation, and globalisation simultaneously
When predicting whether a new political coalition can sustain mobilisation beyond one electoral cycle without Truth 2 and Truth 3
When assessing how a sacred principle ('sovereignty', 'national interest') is being deployed to pre-empt all policy trade-off discussions

title: Why People Rebel
summary: The conditions under which grievance turns into mobilisation, and mobilisation into upheaval.
use_cases: When NPS retirees receive smaller pensions than OPS retirees and relative deprivation, not absolute poverty, drives the political pressure to roll back the reform
When designing a pension, welfare, or subsidy transition that must manage expectations as capabilities change
When Rajasthan and Chhattisgarh roll back NPS and you need to diagnose whether the government raised capabilities or lowered expectations
When a society with mild objective grievances mobilises violently while a poorer society with larger absolute gaps stays stable
When the 2020-21 farmers' protests are driven by fear of future loss, not current deprivation — showing the framework's forward-looking blind spot
When assessing whether a reform's political failure stems from a gap between expectations and capabilities that was left unmanaged

title: Radically Networked Societies
summary: How states should think about populations whose every member is now in real-time contact with every other.
use_cases: When the 2020 CAA protests scaled from campus to nationwide without a central command faster than any party structure could match
When farmers' protests adapted to roadblocks via VPNs and alternative routes while the state's hierarchical response lagged
When a government applies internet shutdowns to a leaderless digital movement and the network finds workarounds
When a spontaneous online campaign is misread as a law-and-order problem rather than a structural speed mismatch
When designing a state counter-mobilisation strategy that matches the feedback speed of a networked opponent
When a viral mobilisation assembles in hours around a hashtag and the bureaucracy is still writing a brief

title: How to Analyse an Analysis
summary: A meta-framework for evaluating the analyses you read — own one before applying any other.
use_cases: When an op-ed advocates a sweeping reform and you need to surface its unstated assumptions before accepting its conclusions
When two sides in a policy debate contradict each other and you need to identify whether they are answering different questions
When a research claim underpins a major spending programme and the methodology needs scrutiny before the money flows
When dissecting a think-tank brief to distinguish genuine reasoning from post-hoc justification of a predetermined position
When a strategic recommendation is backed by analogies and powerful metaphors but lacks explicit premises
When teaching critical reading and needing a structured eight-point checklist to apply to any argument

title: How to Deter Reasonable People from Undesirable Behaviour
summary: The mechanics of deterrence applied to states acting roughly rationally.
use_cases: When Chinese LAC aggression is treated as ideological irrationality rather than a calculable response to incentives, strategic vulnerability, and perceived opportunity
When designing deterrence that raises costs in specific domains where India has tactical advantage rather than defaulting to purely military response
When addressing the systemic factor — absence of settled boundaries — that makes salami-slicing on the LAC attractive to China
When the lottery-vs-reasonableness analogy applies: the question is what makes an adversary's behaviour rational in their context, not whether they are 'crazy'
When shifting from coercive-only deterrence to a richer mix that engages the adversary as a rational actor while maintaining capability to impose costs
When designing counter-terrorism policy that identifies the incentive structure driving recruitment rather than treating recruiters as purely irrational

title: What is a Nation?
summary: The competing answers to a question that policy debates routinely assume is settled.
use_cases: When textbook revision or monument removal debates are framed as historical scholarship but are actually contests over imagined community
When analysing India's civilisational-state narrative as a 'reawakening trope' competing with the 1947 'break from the past' framing
When predicting the three-move sequence a political movement will follow when reimagining national identity
When a constitutional amendment signals departure from foundational values and historians are enlisted to reframe the prior decades as slumber
When assessing whether Nehru's Tryst with Destiny speech deployed Move 1 or Move 2 — and what competing narratives deploy today
When a movement reframes its enemy as 'foreign invaders' to construct an ancient imagined community predating the modern state

title: Terrorism, Poverty, and Education
summary: The actual relationship between these three variables, against the lazy assumption that they are simply linked.
use_cases: When counter-terrorism policy prioritises poverty alleviation and you need to test whether the target population is above or below the socioeconomic threshold
When vocational training or education subsidies are proposed as radicalisation prevention and you need to check what the evidence actually says
When explaining why educated, politicised young men from middling backgrounds are overrepresented in terrorist recruitment pools
When a donor or ministry frames its counter-extremism grant as development aid and the causal chain needs scrutiny
When political information environment and network effects — not income — are the relevant recruitment variables
When a society's growing middle class raises the pool of potential political actors, including violent ones

title: Building Digital Communities
summary: Strategies for making online groups work — and the failure modes to avoid.
use_cases: When designing an online community that must balance openness, quality, and activity without collapsing into toxicity
When a thriving alumni WhatsApp group degrades into spam and forwards as membership scales past a threshold
When deciding whether to add moderation friction — reputation systems, tiered access — to a growing digital group
When explaining why Wikipedia and HackerNews succeeded where most open forums failed
When diagnosing whether a community's quality decline is an openness problem, an activity problem, or both
When designing civic-tech platforms where population loss of bad-faith actors is a desired governance outcome

title: Defence Against the Dark Arts in the Information Age
summary: A working stance against motivated misinformation in everyday reading.
use_cases: When reports of caste violence, crimes against women, or pollution incidents spike and you need to distinguish real increase from expanded moral circle
When a political actor claims 'India is getting worse' and you need to test whether reporting frequency equals prevalence
When industrial pollution makes headlines more often and the question is whether the problem grew or standards rose
When evaluating whether alarm about a social evil is justified by data or inflated by information-theoretic dynamics of media
When China's low corruption-reporting rate is offered as evidence of less corruption rather than less reporting
When designing a three-question diagnostic before concluding that a society is deteriorating on any social dimension

title: Why Large WhatsApp Groups Are So Ineffective
summary: The structural reasons that group-chat scale defeats the very communication it was meant to enable.
use_cases: When a 200-member apartment complex WhatsApp group cannot get a broken road fixed despite unanimous agreement it needs fixing
When diagnosing why the BJP's panna pramukh model (30 voters per person) outperforms a large WhatsApp broadcast group
When organising community mobilisation and deciding whether to split a large group into smaller cells with visible accountability
When a civic group wants to start a clean neighbourhood campaign and needs to choose between one large group and ten cells of eight
When designing a grant-funded community initiative and collective action failures are the likeliest cause of underperformance
When an alumni network asks why 5000 members produce no collective outcomes while a WhatsApp group of 15 arranges everything

title: What the Census Reveals About State and Society
summary: Reading demographic data as a window into the state's grip on the population.
use_cases: When Pakistan delays publishing census results and you need to diagnose whether the cause is weak state capacity or deliberate political suppression
When India's debate over enumerating OBCs in the census illustrates how counting creates political communities and distributional bargains
When Baloch nationalists demand postponing a census until refugees leave — mapping it to Stone's Reason 1 (inclusion/exclusion)
When census delay is offered as evidence of administrative backlog and you need to distinguish it from deliberate obfuscation
When assessing which demographic categories a proposed survey should enumerate and anticipating their downstream political effects
When a 'simple' population count is contested and you need a framework to explain why every number is a political claim

title: The Right & Wrong of Ages Past
summary: . Nevertheless, something that comes often in public debates is the issue of passing moral judgment on someone from the past.
use_cases: When deciding whether to rename an institution or remove a statue based on the historical figure's record
When assessing whether a colonial-era administrator could have known the harm their policies caused
When designing history curricula that neither sanitise the past nor anachronistically condemn it
When a political debate recycles historical grievances and you need to distinguish moral standardism from whataboutery
When evaluating political legacies in contemporary debates using the best standards available at the time
When 'everyone did it then' is used as a moral absolution and needs to be distinguished from genuine standardism

title: How Social Norms Flip
summary: The mechanism by which a norm everyone privately rejects can collapse very quickly.
use_cases: When India's Motor Vehicles Act 2019 raised fines but saw no behaviour change because detection was never increased first
When sequencing enforcement so Step 1 (detection) precedes Step 3 (higher penalties) to shift empirical expectations
When a drunk-driving, seatbelt, or helmet campaign needs to follow the sequence that worked in countries that succeeded
When a behaviour-change programme has failed because it raised fines without making rule-breaking visibly consequential
When state governments offered 90 per cent discounts on challans and you need to diagnose why the norm never shifted
When designing anti-corruption or tax compliance campaigns that must change what people believe others are doing

title: Internet and Politics
summary: How digital networks reshape the basic structures of political life.
use_cases: When a Twitter campaign forces a minister to respond and you need to identify which of the six pathways is at work
When BJP's WhatsApp infrastructure or AAP's volunteer network needs to be mapped to Pathway 5 constituent mobilisation
When a civic-tech app like Swachhata-MoHUA is proposed and you need to anticipate whether backend state capacity will match the frontend
When expatriates resolving passport issues by tweeting at MEA illustrates Pathway 3 direct digital democracy
When assessing whether a new government digital platform will thicken deliberation or enable surveillance (Pathway 6)
When designing a civic-tech intervention that avoids the engagement-maximisation trap of commercial platform incentives

title: Social Norms and Public Policy
summary: Individuals live at the intersection of markets, society, and the State. Markets sometimes fail, and so do the State and society. We know reasonably well about market failures and State failures, but much less about what causes social failures and how to resolve such failures.
use_cases: When open defecation persists despite toilet construction and you need to understand why expectations, not facilities, are the constraint
When Swachh Bharat-style campaigns work in some villages but not others despite identical infrastructure
When dowry, female genital mutilation, or caste endogamy persists even though most participants privately oppose it
When a behaviour-change campaign relies on information and incentives but misses the reference network driving compliance
When sequencing enforcement so that making norm-breaking visible precedes raising penalties
When market interventions and legal bans have both failed and social expectation is the missing variable

title: What's Easy & What's Not?
summary: required for that particular intervention exists, can be built up, or is out of reach. In that sense, it is useful to know  things that can be done easily and the ones that can't, especially when there is dearth of state capacity.
use_cases: When a state that cannot collect taxes reliably proposes industrial policy that demands fine-grained discretion
When Aadhaar-linked welfare delivery works in some states but fails in others due to pre-existing administrative backbone differences
When sequencing institutional development to ensure basic capacity is built before advanced regulatory design is attempted
When diagnosing whether a well-designed policy failed due to design error or due to the state's incapacity to execute
When a reform requires capabilities — data systems, regulatory bandwidth, judicial capacity — that the bureaucracy does not yet have
When comparing why some states implement land reforms successfully while others cannot, using capacity as the explanatory variable

title: The Impossible Trinity of Indian Cities
summary: Affordable housing, easy commute, and pleasant environment — pick any two.
use_cases: When Mumbai, Delhi, and Bengaluru offer economic dynamism and individual liberty but impose killer commutes and unaffordable housing as the hidden price
When Goa or Kerala cities offer livability and liberty but thin economic networks — mapping the trade-off explicitly for a relocation decision
When smaller or Gujarat towns offer economic opportunity and affordability but restrict individual expression through stronger community norms
When someone claims a city offers all three and you need to ask which is being subsidised by invisible costs
When advising a firm on location decisions by mapping the trilemma trade-off for their employee profile and talent needs
When evaluating urban policy that promises improved livability without acknowledging which other corner of the trilemma must be sacrificed

title: Inter-State Water Sharing
summary: A working framework for thinking about transboundary water disputes.
use_cases: When the Kaveri dispute repeats because tribunal-generated allocations produce binary winners and no flexibility for rainfall variability
When a water-surplus state like Karnataka could sell conservation credits to a deficit state like Tamil Nadu rather than fight in court
When designing alternatives to fixed historical entitlements using basic, overdraw, and super-overdraw pricing tiers
When assessing whether a water pricing mechanism will be defeated politically on commodification grounds despite its efficiency
When a river crosses state lines and the current system forces zero-sum legal battles with no mechanism for states to compensate each other
When groundwater exploitation is undermining any surface water allocation agreement and the framework's limits need to be stated explicitly

title: Three Binding Constraints on Technological Progress
summary: A new technology faces three constraints in sequence — technical, economic, and policy. The binding one moves over time, and the slowest-moving determines progress.
use_cases: When crypto's interesting work moved from coding (technological PPF) to exchanges (economic PPF) to litigation and lobbying (policy PPF)
When solar, EVs, drone delivery, or gene editing are stuck and you need to identify whether the binding constraint is engineering, cost, or regulation
When a government subsidy is proposed for a technology whose binding constraint is not economic but regulatory — diagnosing the mismatch
When CRISPR germline editing hits policy resistance before the cost frontier has been crossed — showing the sequence is not always ordered
When allocating R&D budgets vs. regulatory advocacy resources across a portfolio of emerging technologies at different lifecycle stages
When an R&D ministry funds basic research on a technology whose constraint has already moved to policy — redirecting the intervention

title: Moralising is Central to Storytelling
summary: Why every powerful narrative carries an ethical claim — and how to read for it.
use_cases: When deconstructing a reform proposal's political narrative to identify who is the protagonist, who is the villain, and what moral emotion is being activated
When an Overton Window shift requires not more evidence but a more compelling moral story to displace the incumbent narrative
When crafting policy communications that mobilise support without amplifying the rage-and-villains structure that stories naturally produce
When explaining why demonetisation's narrative power outlasted its economic failure — the moral story held even as the data disappointed
When a policy debate is being lost because the opposing side has the better story, not the better evidence
When reading a political speech to separate the moral framing from the policy content and assess which is doing the persuasive work

title: How Social Media Expands Reference Networks
summary: The mechanism by which platforms grow whom we are constantly comparing ourselves to.
use_cases: When a student in Indore compares her life not to classmates but to curated strangers in London — and status anxiety follows
When explaining why online political movements feel intensely personal even when participants have never met
When platform regulation focuses only on content moderation but misses the deeper structural rewiring of social comparison
When performative outrage and identity-based solidarity intensify as comparator pools expand from dozens to millions
When assessing why a purely domestic policy issue acquires global resonance and cross-border amplification
When a social media policy produces unintended consequences because it treats the platform as a faster newspaper, not a rewired reference network

title: Xi's Trilemma
summary: The three-way constraint that shapes the strategic options of contemporary China.
use_cases: When China's property market collapses and stimulus packages disappoint — tracing the failure to the party-dominance constraint that blocks private investment
When forecasting whether China will escalate Taiwan or South China Sea aggression as domestic growth weakens and nationalist spectacle rises
When India's strategists need a framework for why Chinese external behaviour will worsen as economic performance deteriorates
When assessing why tech-sector crackdowns, anti-corruption campaigns, and 'common prosperity' rhetoric undermine the very growth that funds party legitimacy
When evaluating whether any state-led development model that suppresses private capital can simultaneously claim global technological leadership
When predicting which of the three goals Xi is likely to downgrade first and what signals would indicate that choice

title: Building Models
summary: The basic craft of constructing simple, useful models of complex situations.
use_cases: When 'higher MSPs help farmers' is asserted without specifying who captures the benefit, at what fiscal cost, and with what second-order effects on land rents
When BSNL's ₹1.64 lakh crore revival package pursues two independent targets (rural connectivity and indigenous 4G) with one instrument
When a policy claim lacks causal mechanisms and needs to be made testable through deliberate simplification
When farm law, GST, or labour reform debate proceeds on political instinct and a causal model is needed to make the dispute resolvable
When teaching students to move from slogans to structured reasoning by isolating the variables that actually matter
When diagnosing whether a proposed intervention has unexamined second-order effects before implementation begins

title: Prohibition and Morality
summary: The relationship between legal restriction and ethical claim — and why the two diverge so often.
use_cases: When Bihar or Gujarat's alcohol prohibition produces hooch tragedies and bootlegger networks — applying the diagnostic test before the ban
When the Gold (Control) Act of 1968 is the historical mirror for any new ban on a widely desired good
When evaluating whether a proposed ban on a victimless activity will generate enforcement machinery more harmful than the activity itself
When deciding between outright ban and regulatory alternatives — taxation, licensing, harm reduction — using prohibition's failure modes as the benchmark
When moral inversion is visible: the people society respects are those skilled at evading the law, not those who comply
When assessing whether legal restriction and ethical claim are aligned for a new proposed prohibition or whether they diverge

title: Social Media's Rule of Three
summary: A pattern in how attention, virality, and reaction interact on networked platforms.
use_cases: When a fringe religious or political position becomes mainstream within weeks and you need to identify which of the three mechanisms is driving it
When the DRASTIC group's COVID-origins shift needs to be explained by reference network expansion, Overton stretch, and virality of surprising content
When designing counter-extremism strategies that address the structural pull toward shock, not just platform content moderation
When explaining why WhatsApp forwards carry a religious claim further than a considered op-ed on the same topic
When platform regulation proposes to fix echo chambers but misses the two other mechanisms also radicalising the feed
When assessing why moderation is invisible and extremity is rewarded — applying the information-theory logic of scarce attention

title: Hal Varian's Tips on Building an Economic Model
summary: Practical advice from a working economist on how to make a model that earns its keep.
use_cases: When a researcher's first instinct is to start with a literature review and risks ending up with someone else's model
When a newspaper column or a bad argument sparks a policy question worth formalising before consulting what others have written
When a half-formed policy intuition needs to be stress-tested by presenting it to a sceptical seminar before it hardens into a draft
When Indian policy research produces literature-heavy papers that replicate known results rather than model new problems
When distinguishing which step of model-building — ideation, construction, or exposition — is the current bottleneck
When a model is elegant on paper but cannot survive a sceptical public audience — using the seminar as the first filter

title: How to Build a Good 2x2 Matrix
summary: The discipline that separates a useful 2x2 from a decorative one.
use_cases: When a Takshashila scenario exercise on Iran's global status needs two axes that are genuinely independent and uncertain, not correlated proxies
When a strategy team produces a 2x2 with 'good vs bad' on both axes and needs a rigorous axis-selection process
When structuring uncertainty about India's energy mix in 2035 by ranking driving forces by importance and uncertainty before picking axes
When preparing an organisation for multiple futures by deriving leading indicators that signal which quadrant is emerging
When the first draft of a scenario matrix produces only two populated diagonals — diagnosing correlated rather than independent axes
When a lazy matrix embeds the analyst's preferred conclusion in the 'desirable' quadrant and needs to be challenged
